matter.title: Olivia B. Ellsworth v. Maria Q. Fairbanks
matter.summary: Olivia B. Ellsworth was injured in a rear-end collision at Interstate 295 northbound near Exit 7 and sues the at-fault driver Maria Q. Fairbanks (and the driver's employer Coastal Outfitters LLC) for negligence, claiming a fractured wrist and ribs and roughly $211745 in medical bills.

fact_pattern.summary: Olivia B. Ellsworth was injured in a rear-end collision at Interstate 295 northbound near Exit 7 and sues the at-fault driver Maria Q. Fairbanks (and the driver's employer Coastal Outfitters LLC) for negligence, claiming a fractured wrist and ribs and roughly $211745 in medical bills.
fact_pattern.narrative:
On 2024-05-30, Olivia B. Ellsworth was lawfully operating a motor vehicle at Interstate 295 northbound near Exit 7 when Maria Q. Fairbanks caused a rear-end collision. Olivia suffered a fractured wrist and ribs and was transported for emergency care; Olivia's vehicle sustained roughly $33513 in damage and was towed from the scene.

Olivia alleges that Maria drove negligently, breaching the duty of reasonable care owed to other motorists, and that this breach proximately caused the collision and resulting injuries. To the extent Maria was operating a commercial vehicle in the course of employment, Olivia alleges that Coastal Outfitters LLC is vicariously liable under respondeat superior and independently negligent in entrusting and supervising the vehicle.

As a result of the collision, Olivia has incurred approximately $211745 in medical specials and about $43175 in lost wages, and continues to experience pain and functional limitation. The defense disputes the nature and extent of the injuries and contends that comparative fault should reduce any recovery. The matter was filed in Sagadahoc County Superior Court and is in active discovery.
undisputed_facts: A rear-end collision occurred at Interstate 295 northbound near Exit 7 on 2024-05-30 involving the parties' vehicles.
Olivia B. Ellsworth received medical treatment following the collision.
The investigating officer responded to the scene and prepared a crash report.
disputed_facts: Which driver was at fault for the rear-end collision, and in what proportion.
Whether the fractured wrist and ribs was caused by the collision or by a pre-existing condition.
The reasonable value of the plaintiff's past and future medical care and lost earnings.
Whether the plaintiff's own comparative fault should reduce or bar recovery under 14 M.R.S. § 156.

timeline.event: The rear-end collision occurred at Interstate 295 northbound near Exit 7; Olivia was injured.
Complaint for negligence filed in Bath Superior Court.

third_parties.name: Samuel Q. Inglewood
third_parties.relevance: Treated the plaintiff's injuries and can testify to diagnosis, causation, and prognosis.

third_parties.name: John W. Poe
third_parties.relevance: Responded to the scene and authored the official crash report and diagram.

third_parties.name: Cobblestone Partners Co.
third_parties.relevance: Towed the plaintiff's vehicle and holds the post-collision inspection and storage records.

third_parties.name: Summit Analytics Corp.
third_parties.relevance: Carries the at-fault driver's liability policy and controls the defense and any settlement within limits.

third_parties.name: Riverbend Studios LLC
third_parties.relevance: Carries the plaintiff's policy, including uninsured/underinsured motorist coverage, and paid medical-payments benefits.

exchanges.question: Walk me through how the crash happened.
exchanges.answer: I was driving normally at Interstate 295 northbound near Exit 7 when it became a rear-end collision. I never had a chance to avoid it. The police came and wrote it up.

exchanges.question: What injuries did you suffer, and how are you now?
exchanges.answer: I was diagnosed with a fractured wrist and ribs. I have been in treatment ever since and still have pain and trouble doing things I used to do easily.

exchanges.question: What has your medical care looked like?
exchanges.answer: Emergency care, imaging, follow-ups, and therapy. The bills are already around $211745, and my doctor says I may need more care.

exchanges.question: Has this affected your ability to work?
exchanges.answer: Yes. I missed a lot of work and lost about $43175 in wages. I am worried about whether I can keep up long term.

exchanges.question: Is the other driver blaming you at all?
exchanges.answer: Their insurer is hinting I was partly at fault, but I had the right of way. I do not think any of this was my fault.

exchanges.question: What outcome are you hoping for?
exchanges.answer: Fair compensation for my medical bills, lost wages, and what I have been through. I will go to trial if I have to, but a fair settlement is fine.

goals: Establish the defendant's negligence and full liability for the rear-end collision.
Recover compensation for past and future medical care, lost wages, and pain and suffering.
Maximize recovery against available liability and underinsured-motorist coverage.
priorities: Proving causation linking the fractured wrist and ribs to the collision.
Documenting the full economic and non-economic loss.
desired_outcomes: A judgment or settlement compensating the plaintiff's full damages.
A liability finding (or strong settlement posture) unreduced by comparative fault.
non_starters: A nuisance-value settlement that ignores the future medical needs.

constraints.budget: Contingency-fee representation; costs advanced and recovered from any judgment.
constraints.timeline: Wants the matter resolved before the close of discovery if a fair offer is made.

issues.issue: Whether the plaintiff's comparative fault reduces or bars recovery
sub_questions: Did the plaintiff exercise reasonable care?
If fault is shared, what is each party's percentage?
issues.client_position: The plaintiff bears no fault and any reduction is unwarranted.

issues.issue: Entitlement to punitive damages for the conduct alleged
sub_questions: Does the evidence show malice or reckless indifference?

issues.issue: Whether the employer is vicariously liable for the driver's conduct
issues.governing_law: Respondeat superior; common law (vicarious liability and negligent entrustment)
sub_questions: Was the driver acting within the scope of employment?
Did the employer negligently entrust the vehicle?
issues.client_position: The employer is liable for its driver's negligence committed on the job.

sub_questions: Do the at-fault driver's limits fall short of the plaintiff's damages?
What UM/UIM benefits are available to the plaintiff?

issues.issue: Liens and subrogation — MaineCare and workers'-comp claims against the recovery
sub_questions: MaineCare's § 14 lien is NOT reduced for a pro-rata share of attorney fees, and requires written notice before settlement and at least 10 days before filing — has that been done, and how does the no-fee-reduction rule change the net to the plaintiff?
A workers'-comp lien under 39-A M.R.S. § 107 creates a de facto three-party settlement, and its 30-day written-demand subrogation clock can force a premature suit.
issues.notes: Overbroad-risk: the WC lien is NOT simply total benefits paid — the employer bears proportionate litigation costs, which reduces it. Private health-insurer subrogation is policy-language dependent and UNVERIFIED here. [confounder:liens-subrogation-mainecare-wc; VERIFIED — 22 M.R.S. § 14 MaineCare no-fee-reduction lien; 39-A M.R.S. § 107 WC lien; private health-insurer subrogation UNVERIFIED]

expert_opinions.topic: Cause and mechanics of the collision
expert_opinions.opinion: The physical evidence shows the defendant's vehicle caused the impact; the plaintiff could not have avoided it.
expert_opinions.basis: Crash report, scene measurements, vehicle damage, and event-data-recorder download.

expert_opinions.topic: Diagnosis, causation, and prognosis of the injuries
expert_opinions.opinion: The plaintiff's fractured wrist and ribs was caused by the collision and will require ongoing care.
expert_opinions.basis: Examination, imaging, and the course of treatment.

expert_opinions.opinion: The forces involved were insufficient to cause the claimed injuries.

evidence.title: Wage-loss documentation
evidence.description: Employer letter and pay records substantiating lost earnings.

evidence.title: Scene and vehicle photographs
evidence.description: Photographs of the vehicles, damage, debris field, and roadway.

evidence.title: Police crash report and diagram
evidence.description: The investigating officer's official report, narrative, and scene diagram.

evidence.title: 911 call transcript
evidence.description: Transcript and audio of the emergency call reporting the collision.

evidence.title: Vehicle repair estimate
evidence.description: Itemized estimate or total-loss valuation for the plaintiff's vehicle.

evidence.title: Medical records and itemized bills
evidence.description: Treatment records and billing for the plaintiff's injuries.

evidence.title: Defendant's cell-phone records
evidence.description: Call and text logs around the time of the collision relevant to distraction.

amounts.label: Property damage
amounts.basis: Vehicle repair estimate or total-loss value.

causes_of_action.relief_sought: Compensatory damages for medical expenses, lost wages, and pain and suffering

elements: Conduct within the scope of employment
Harm of the type the statute guards against

affirmative_defenses.basis: The claimed injuries are pre-existing or degenerative and were not caused by the collision.
The defendant faced an unforeseen emergency not of the defendant's making.
The plaintiff's own negligence caused or contributed to the collision. 14 M.R.S. § 156.
The action was not commenced within the limitations period. 14 M.R.S. § 752.

counterclaims.relief: Damages for the defendant's own vehicle repair costs allegedly caused by the plaintiff.

discovery.description: Subpoena for the policy, declarations, and claim file.
Admissions on fault, right-of-way, and authenticity of the crash report.
Defense medical examination of the plaintiff.
Deposition of the treating physician on causation.

motions.title: Motion to compel the independent medical examination
motions.relief: An order compelling the plaintiff to attend a defense medical examination.

motions.title: Motion in limine to exclude the accident reconstruction
motions.relief: Exclusion of the plaintiff's reconstruction expert testimony.

motions.title: Motion to bifurcate liability and damages
motions.relief: Separate trials on liability and damages.

docket.entry: Complaint and demand for jury trial filed.
Defendants' answer and affirmative defenses filed.
Scheduling order entered; discovery deadline set.
Order compelling production of the defendant's phone records.
Pre-suit demand letter sent to the defendant's liability insurer.
Defense medical examination completed; expert disclosures exchanged.
Complaint for negligence filed and served after the demand went unmet.
Defendant served an Answer pleading comparative negligence (14 M.R.S. § 156) and failure to mitigate; no counterclaim.
Partial summary judgment on liability taken under advisement.
Defendant moved in limine to exclude the plaintiff's treating physician's causation opinion.
Mediation held; case continues toward trial.

procedure.event: Pre-suit demand letter sent to the defendant's liability insurer.
Complaint for negligence filed and served after the demand went unmet.
Defendant served an Answer pleading comparative negligence (14 M.R.S. § 156) and failure to mitigate; no counterclaim.
Defendant moved in limine to exclude the plaintiff's treating physician's causation opinion.
procedure.deadline_rule: M.R. Civ. P. 7(b)(1)(A) — opposition within 21 days; M.R. Evid. 702/703 causation foundation.
